coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon/graphitic CQDs composites with 98.8% microporosity as a highly stable electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Shu, Chenyang
Guo, Chaozhong
Xu, Xinru
Sun, Lingtao
Liu, Yao
Chen, Hongdian
Xu, Chuanlan
Jin, Rong
Si, Yujun
Chen, Haifeng
coredata.dc:description:
Designing micropore-rich carbon materials with large specific surface area (SSA) as a carbon-based oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) electrocatalyst is still frontier but remains challenging in catalysis of zinc-air batteries. Here we propose a new strategy for synthesis of nitrogen atoms co-doped carbon sheets and carbon quantum dots (N-C@CQDs) with an SSA of 1485.3 m2 g−1 and 98.8 % micro-porosity from α-cellulose via using a complex salt (ZnCl2-NH4Cl) with low eutectic point (179 °C) as a pore regulator and K2FeO4 as a graphitization catalyst, which can serve as an active and stable carbon-based ORR electrocatalyst. The role of K2FeO4 not only promotes graphitization but also oxidizes the large-sized graphitic carbon particles into a huge amount of uniformly distributed CQDs. This catalyst has exhibited a comparable ORR activity and superior stability to the Pt/C, but a Zn-air battery assembled with the former has delivered a higher peak power density (214 mW cm−2) and a larger gravimetric energy density (ED) of (959 Wh
k g
Z n
- 1
), especially in which it also displays an outstanding catalytic durability with 98.7 % ED retention during a ∼110 h continuous discharge. This work provides a new pathway for design of high-performance carbon-based ORR catalysts in various energy devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon
Carbon quantum dots
Electrocatalysis
Oxygen reduction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ORR catalytic activity evaluation of the catalysts
Zinc-air battery tests using the catalysts

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 22275026 and 21805024), the Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of Chongqing, China (Grant No. CSTB2023NSCQJQX0032), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China (Grant Nos. cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0783 and cstc2018jcyjAX0461), the Scientific and Technological Research Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (Grant Nos.KJQN202001335, KJZD-K202101303, KJQN202101313, KJQN202201319), the Special Postdoctoral Foundation of Chongqing City (Grant No. 2022CQBSHTB3070) and the Research Program of Chongqing University of Arts and Sciences (Grant No. R2018SDQ15).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustation of the preparation of N-C@CQDs. (b-c) TEM images of N-C@CQDs. (d) HR-TEM of N-C@CQDs. (e) BET spectra of N-C@CQDs (inset: micropore ratio of the prepared catalysts). (f) Elemental mapping of N-C@CQDs (C, N and O).

(a-b) XRD patterns of the prepared catalysts (Fig. 2b is a partially enlarged view of Fig. 2a). (c) Raman spectra of the prepared catalysts. (d) C 1s XPS spectrum of N-C@CQDs. (e) N 1s XPS spectrum of Cel-900-CS s. (f) N 1s XPS spectrum of N-C@CQDs.

(a) CV curves of the prepared catalysts in O2-saturated or N2-saturated 0.1 M KOH at 50 mV s−1. (b) LSV curves of the prepared catalysts, and Pt/C. (c) LSV curves of N-C@CQDs at different rotational speeds; the inset is the K-L plot. (d) Corresponding electron transfer numbers and H2O2 yields for N-C@CQDs and Pt/C; inset is the corresponding I d and I r for N-C@CQDs and Pt/C. (e) Tafel slopes for the prepared catalysts, and Pt/C. (f) Double-layer capacitances of the prepared catalysts.

(a) Schematic diagram of a zinc-air battery. (b) Open-circuit voltages of batteries. (c) Polariztion curves and power density curves. (d) Discharge curves at different current densities. (e) Discharge curves of N-C@CQDs and Pt/C at 50 mA cm−2. (f) Long-time galvanostatic discharge curves at 50 mA cm−2, in which the recharging is realized by changing the Zn anode.

(a) Charge-discharge curves of N-C@CQDs@RuO2 and Pt/C@RuO2 at 10 mA cm−2. (b) Charge and discharge polarization curves and power density curves at 10 mA cm−2 (c) Comparison of the performance of primary zinc-air cells assembled with N-C@CQDs and other reported non-precious metal carbon-based catalysts.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon/graphitic CQDs composites with 98.8% microporosity as a highly stable electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We propose a new strategy for synthesis of nitrogen atoms co-doped carbon sheets and carbon quantum dots (N-C@CQDs) with an SSA of 1485.3 m2 g−1 and 98.8% micro-porosity from α-cellulose via using a complex salt (ZnCl2-NH4Cl) with low eutectic point (179 °C) as a pore regulator and K2FeO4 as a graphitization catalyst, which can serve as an active and stable ORR electrocatalyst.

Designing micropore-rich carbon materials with large specific surface area (SSA) as a carbon-based oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) electrocatalyst is still frontier but remains challenging in catalysis of zinc-air batteries. Here we propose a new strategy for synthesis of nitrogen atoms co-doped carbon sheets and carbon quantum dots (N-C@CQDs) with an SSA of 1485.3 m2 g−1 and 98.8 % micro-porosity from α-cellulose via using a complex salt (ZnCl2-NH4Cl) with low eutectic point (179 °C) as a pore regulator and K2FeO4 as a graphitization catalyst, which can serve as an active and stable carbon-based ORR electrocatalyst. The role of K2FeO4 not only promotes graphitization but also oxidizes the large-sized graphitic carbon particles into a huge amount of uniformly distributed CQDs. This catalyst has exhibited a comparable ORR activity and superior stability to the Pt/C, but a Zn-air battery assembled with the former has delivered a higher peak power density (214 mW cm−2) and a larger gravimetric energy density (ED) of (959 Wh
k g
Z n
- 1
), especially in which it also displays an outstanding catalytic durability with 98.7 % ED retention during a ∼110 h continuous discharge. This work provides a new pathway for design of high-performance carbon-based ORR catalysts in various energy devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: There is a rising focus on the advancement of clean and renewable energy sources due to the increased energy consumption and the constant birth of advanced technologies.[1–4] Among the energy conversion devices, zinc-air batteries (ZABs) are being intensively investigated, ascribing to the many merits such as high theoretical energy density up to 1086Wh/kg, safety and reliability, and high efficiency and cleanliness.[5–7].

Currently, the commercialization of ZABs is blocked by the low energy utilization efficiency and poor cyclic stability of the batteries. Oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) occurs on the air electrode during the discharging process of the batteries.[8–10] The sluggish kinetics and complicated mechanism of ORR call for the development of ORR catalysts with robust activity and excellent reliability. Although Pt-based catalysts exhibit superior activity, the large-scale application is not accepted due to the high cost and limited reserves of the precious metal.[11–13] It is a significant request to substitute the Pt-based catalysts with low-cost materials such as transition metal oxides and nitrogen doped carbon catalysts (M−N/C, M = Fe, Co, Ni, Mn, etc.).[14–15] Wang et al. synthesized an edge-hosted Mn-N4-C12 coordination supported on N-doped defective carbon (Mn1/NDC) catalyst, ultralow-temperature and high-capacity SS ZABs without organic solvent were achieved by combining an edge-hosted atomic Mn site cathode with a stable Zn|SSE interface.[16] In a follow-up work, Wang et al. combined electrolyte engineering and atomic Mo site catalysts, atomic Mo site significantly accelerated the oxygen evolution kinetics of α-Co(OH)2 nanosheets, achieving a fast-charging ZAB.[17].

The rapid development of carbon-based catalysts for ORR has attracted lots of attention owing to their stable physical properties and good chemical activities.[18,19] Various types of carbon materials, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, and carbon nanofibers, have been explored as ORR electrocatalysts with high-performances due to their high electron transport, scalable form, and convenience.[20–22].

Biomass materials have been reported as the hopeful precursors for the preparation of porous carbon, such as sugar, lignocellulose, animal tissue, natural balsam fiber, haddock bark, and dandelion seeds, etc.[23] A shortage of biomasses is the complicated compositions and low content of the hetero-elements.[24] Supplying additional hetero-element sources is a practical way to increase the doping/loading amount and further enhance the catalytic activity.[25–27] Although it is feasible to acquire porous carbon materials from biomass, the microstructures are most predominantly mesoporous or macroporous, or even disordered, which limits their performance in the electrochemical process and further investigation on the methodology is still required. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional carbon materials with improved electrical conductivity, adjustable bandwidth gaps, and excellent optoelectronic features.[28] CQDs are of considerable interest as ORR catalysts owing to their excellent electrical conductivity, oxygen-functional groups, highly exposed surfaces, edge effects, and hydrophilicity.[29,30] CQDs can also act as active centers to increase electrocatalytic capacity.[31] However, the enhanced performance is not always inevitable by the utilization of CQDs. For example, Jo synthesized a nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) decorated platinum (Pt) nanoparticles (Pt@N-CQDs) as an oxygen reduction reaction catalyst.[32] However, its half-wave potential and ultimate current density are significantly lower than those of the Pt/C catalyst. Hence, it is valuable to efficiently couple carbon quantum dots with biomass carbon-based catalysts to prepare ORR catalysts with low cost and high performance.

In this work, a nitrogen doped carbon-based catalyst (N-C@CQDs) was prepared by anchoring carbon quantum dots on two-dimensional graphitized carbon sheets with high microporosity. We have demonstrated the excellent ORR electrochemical performance of the catalyst on activity and stability. Further studies showed that the synthesized catalysts N-C@CQDs exhibited better performance than Pt/C catalysts on practical zinc-air batteries.

Zinc chloride (ZnCl2) were purchased from Chengdu Cologne Chemical Co., Ltd. Potassium perferrate (VI) (K2FeO4) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Ammonium chloride (NH4Cl) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. α-cellulose (90 μm) was obtained by Shanghai Titan Science Co., Ltd.

0.1 g of K2FeO4, 1 g of α-cellulose (90 μm), 1 g of ZnCl2 and 1 g of NH4Cl were dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water and stirred for 12 h. The precursor Zn-N-cellulose was obtained after drying in at 60 °C for 12 h. The precursor Zn-N-cellulose was ground for 30 min and then pyrolized at 900 °C for 2 h under nitrogen atmosphere at a rate of 5 °C min−1. After the temperature cooled to room temperature, the resulting powder was immersed in 60 mL of 0.1 M H2SO4 solution at 80 °C for 8 h to remove the unstable impurities. After filtering, washing, and drying, the collected powder was further calcined at 900 °C under nitrogen for 1 h to obtain the catalyst N-C@CQDs. As references, Cel-900 (from pure cellulose without activators), Cel-900-PF (without ZnCl2 and NH4Cl), and Cel-900-CS (without K2FeO4) were constructed by the similar procedure.

The samples were observed morphologically using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi UHR S4800). A transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai-G2 F30) was used to further investigate the morphology, crystal structure, electron selective diffraction, EDS energy spectra and lattice fringes. The phase structure was measured using an X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, XD-6) at a scan rate of 5° min−1. The defect level of carbon in the prepared catalysts was determined by Raman spectroscopy. The specific surface area was measured by a Micromeritics ASAP 2010 analyser. The content of Fe was also determined by inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometry (Agilent, ICP-OES 730).

The electrochemical measurements were carried out on a typical three-electrode system supported by the electrochemical workstation CHI760E (Shanghai Chenhua Instruments Co., Ltd., China). The device used to assemble the ZABs was acquired from Changsha Spring and the battery performance were tested on a battery tester (CT-4008, Neware, China). The electrochemical measurements are introduced in detail in the Supporting Information.

The preparation of catalyst N-C@CQDs was illustrated in Fig. 1 a. In brief, the catalyst is prepared by mixing the complex salt ZnCl2-NH4Cl as a pore regulator, K2FeO4 as a graphitization catalyst with commercial cellulose in water, and then dried, followed by high-temperature pyrolysis. The morphology of the N-C@CQDs catalyst was observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Figure S1, the N-C@CQDs catalyst is composed of two-dimensional graphite sheets. The TEM images in Fig. 1b-c clearly show that the graphite sheets are decorated by uniformly dispersed quantum dots about 10 nm in size. The high resolution TEM image in Fig. 1d indicates the existence of lattice fringes with 0.31 nm of d-spacing, revealing the generation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in the catalyst.[33] More TEM and selected area electron diffraction images are available in Fig. S2-4. Based on the results of N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm measurements, the N-C@CQDs exhibits a sharply increased adsorption quantity at relatively low N2 pressures (p/p 0 < 0.1) and delayed cycling at higher N2 pressures (p/p 0 > 0.4) as shown in Fig. 1e, reflecting the typical type I isotherm with micropores with the dominated sizes below 2 nm. This is also evidenced by the corresponding BJH pore size distribution curve for N-C@CQDs (Figure S5), in which the proportion of micropores is as high as 98.8 % (also listed in the inset of Fig. 1e). The Isotherms and corresponding BJH pore size distributions of Cel-900-PF (without ZnCl2 and NH4Cl) and Cel-900-CS (without K2FeO4) are presented in Figure S6. As observed, the Cel-900-PF shows the characteristic of type IV isotherm dominated by mesopores and micropores, while Cel-900-CS still shows the characteristic of type I isotherm. The difference on the pore size distributions of the three materials indicates that the pore structure can be well regulated by ZnCl2 and NH4Cl to favor the formation of micropores and to contribute the greater specific surface area. As listed in Fig. 1e and Figure S6, the BET specific surface area of N-C@CQDs, Cel-900-CS and Cel-900-PF is 1485.3 m2 g−1,1355.8 m2 g−1 and 545.15 m2 g−1, respectively. The greater specific surface area can provide more active sites exposed. Moreover, the elemental mapping analysis confirms the even distribution of C, N, and O on N-C@CQDs (Fig. 1f), which indicates the successful doping of N. The doping of N can enhance the conductivity on CQDs to obtain higher performance ORR activity.[32].

The phase of the catalyst was analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2 (a-b), no obvious diffraction peaks can be observed in the XRD pattern of Cel-900-CS, revealing the amorphous state of the graphite sheets in the catalyst as shown in Figure S1. With the addition of K2FeO4 into the precursor, the distinct peak of (002) facet for graphite (PDF 75–2078) appears at 26.6°. It is attributed to the fact that K2FeO4 produces Fe(OH)3 during pyrolysis which is reduced to Fe species at a high temperature. The Fe species can promote the graphitization to generate the microcrystalline carbon during pyrolysis [34]. After acid washing and further pyrolysis, all three samples were free of other spurious peaks, indicating that these catalysts are pure carbon materials. The ICP-OES results show that the mass percentage of Fe in N-C@CQDs is only 0.08 %, which further proves the conclusion (Table S1).

The defect level of carbon in the prepared catalysts was evaluated by Raman spectroscopy and displayed in Fig. 2c, in which the intensity ratio I D/I G of D and G bands can reflect the degree of defect in carbon materials[35]. It can be seen that the intensity ratio I D/I G of disordered sp3 carbon band and perfect sp2 graphite band in N-C@CQDs (1.06) is higher than that in Cel-900-CS (1.03) and Cel-900-PF (1.02), suggesting the higher defect level of N-C@CQDs from the doping of carbon quantum dots and the possible higher ORR performance of N-C@CQDs catalyst. The Raman spectroscopy results demonstrate that the complex salt activator ZnCl2-NH4Cl can introduce more defects as the active sites in N-C@CQDs, being responsible for the superior ORR performance of this catalyst.

The surface composition and bond structure of Cel-900-PF, Cel-900-CS, and N-C@CQDs were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Figure S7 and Table S2, C, N, and O are presented in the three catalysts, but the content of Fe is too low to be checked. As shown in Fig. 2e and Figure S8, the C 1s XPS spectrum can be fitted by three peaks at 284.8, 285.8, and 288.9 eV, corresponding to the C = C, C–N, and O–C = O structures, respectively, and verifying the successful doping of nitrogen.[36] The XPS spectra of N 1s can be classified into three peaks at 398.3, 401.1, and 403.2 eV, corresponding to the pyridinic nitrogen, graphitic nitrogen, and nitrogen oxide, respectively (Fig. 2e-f).[37] The degree of graphitization of the activated carbon has an important influence on the microstructure and surface chemistry of the prepared carbon quantum dots, which have a large size, highly ordered sp2 hybridized carbon planes, and are rich in surface functional groups.[38] The analysis of XPS data show that the atomic ratio of graphitic nitrogen in catalyst N-C@CQDs is up to 55.4 %, as shown in Table S3, but the graphitic nitrogen content in N-C@CQDs is higher than in Cel-900-CS (Table S4), proving that the addition of K2FeO4 is helpful to the formation of the graphitized CQDs in the N-C@CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ORR catalytic activity evaluation of the catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The ORR performance of the catalysts was investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) in 0.1 M KOH solution at room temperature. Fig. 3 a compares the CVs of the Cel-900, Cel-900-PF, Cel-900-CS, and N-C@CQDs in N2 or O2 saturated electrolyte. As observed, an ORR peak appears on the CV curve of the catalyst with the order of N-C@CQDs (0.82 V) > Cel-900-CS (0.78 V) > Cel-900-PF (0.67 V) > Cel-900 (0.55 V), showing the better ORR activity can be obtained with the synergy of salt activator ZnCl2-NH4Cl (179 °C) and the crystal promoter K2FeO4.

For the further investigation, the ORR activity of the prepared catalysts was measured by LSV from which the ORR half wave potential (E 1/2) and limit diffusion current density (j d) can be obtained. As presented in Fig. 3b, CEL-900 (from the directly pyrolyzed pure cellulose) shows the worse ORR activity with 0.57 V of E 1/2 and lower j d. With the promotion of K2FeO4, the E 1/2 of Cel-900-PF is moved to 0.65 V, revealing the positive effect of K2FeO4 on the formation of ORR active sites. The large number of micropores can significantly increase the ORR activity of Cel-900-CS with an E 1/2 value of 0.80 V due to the use of NH4Cl-ZnCl2 complex salt as an activator. The E 1/2 for the N-C@CQDs is further boosted to 0.85 V while NH4Cl-ZnCl2 and K2FeO4 were both used to optimize the structure of catalyst, being comparable to the property of commercial Pt/C catalyst and exhibiting the superior ORR activity. This can be attributed to the use of ZnCl2-NH4Cl complex salt as a pore regulator, which dissolves and dehydrates the cellulose during pyrolyzing, and leaving a nitrogen-doped carbon skeleton containing ZnCl2 to form a large number of nitrogen-doped microporous structures after removal of ZnCl2 by acid washing. Thereby, the specific surface area of the catalyst can be remarkably increased to favor the adsorption capacity and mass transfer rate of the catalyst and improve the ORR activity of the catalyst. Furthermore, the synergistic effect of the complex salt NH4Cl-ZnCl2 can reduce the vapor pressure of ZnCl2 and lower the eutectic point of the complex salt, which also reduces the evaporation loss of ZnCl2. Compared with other composite salts such as ZnCl2-SnCl2, ZnCl2-FeCl3, etc., ZnCl2-NH4Cl composite salt is low-cost and environmentally friendly, and does not have an affinity to the wall of the equipment during the activation process, which means that it will not stick to the wall. In addition, the formation of a large number of uniformly distributed graphitized carbon quantum dots by the oxidation of K2FeO4 leads to an increase in electronegativity on the carbon lattice and the introduction of oxygen-containing functional groups on the carbon quantum dots both increase the catalytic activity of the catalyst. These are the keys to the catalyst to exhibit a similar performance to that of the Pt/C catalyst. As a major factor to affect the graphitization and nitrogen doping, the effect of pyrolyzing temperature was investigated by preparing the N-C@CQDs at 800 °C, 900 °C and 1000 °C. The LSV measurement (Figure S9) indicates that N-C@CQDs obtained at 900 °C shows the more positive half-wave potential and greater limit diffusion current.

To explore the dynamics of ORR on the catalysts, LSV was tested at different rotation speeds. It can be seen that the limit current density increases with rotation speed due to more dissolved oxygen in the electrolyte being available to participate in the reaction (Fig. 3c). The Koutecky-Levich (K-L) plots show that the ORR reaction on the N-C@CQDs catalyst satisfies the four-electron pathway. This can be ascribed to the large number of microporous structures which increase the mass transfer rate and the excellent electrical conductivity of the graphitized carbon quantum dots. To further verify the four-electron reaction pathway, rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) voltammetry was carried out and the H2O2 yield during ORR was collected. As shown in the Fig. 3d, the average electron transfer number of 3.95 and an average H2O2 yield of 2.55 % of N-C@CQDs are both comparable to those of the Pt/C catalyst. Fig. 3e illustrates the Tafel slope of 55 mV dec−1 for N-C@CQDs, lower than Pt/C (62 mV dec−1), Cel-900 (68.6 mV dec−1), Cel-900-PF (67 mV dec−1), and Cel-900-CS (56 mV dec−1), revealing the fast kinetics on N-C@CQDs. The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) in the non-Faraday potential region was evaluated by the double layer capacitance (C dl) fitted from the CVs at series of scan rates. As presented in Fig. 3f and Figure S10, N-C@CQDs shows 94.5 mF cm−2 of C dl, which is greater than Cel-900-CS (92.2 mF cm−2), Cel-900-PF (25.7 mF cm−2), and Cel-900 (15.7 mF cm−2), providing the more active sites to ensure the better ORR performance of the N-C@CQDs.

The stability of N-C@CQDs was investigated by an accelerated aging test from CV scanning. As presented in Figure S11, the half wave potential E 1/2 of N-C@CQDs just negatively moved at 2 mV after 5000 cycles CV scan, being apparently lower than the 22 mV of Pt/C catalyst. Chronoamperometry (i-t) was also measured to assess the stability. As displayed in Figure S12, 95.5 % of current retention on N-C@CQDs is higher than the 94.3 % of commercial Pt/C after 10000 s of test, revealing the outstanding stability of the N-C@CQDs catalyst. 1 mL of 3 mol/L methanol solution was also added to the electrolyte solution at 400 s and 700 s during the chronoamperometry. As shown in Figure S13, the current density on the commercial Pt/C catalyst is sharply decreased with the addition of methanol. Whereas no current decay is observed on N-C@CQDs, further reflecting its excellent methanol resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zinc-air battery tests using the catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the performance of the catalyst in a practical application, a primary zinc-air battery was assembled by coating N-C@CQDs on the air electrode with 0.5 mg cm−2 of loading, and using zinc sheet as anode and 6 M KOH solution as the electrolyte as schemed in Fig. 4 a. As presented in Fig. 4b, the open-circuit voltage of N-C@CQDs-based battery is up to ∼ 1.55 V, being comparable to 1.55 V of Pt/C-based battery, and a diode screen can be lightened by two series of the battery. LSV was tested on the battery. As shown in Fig. 4c, the voltage of N-C@CQDs is higher than that of Pt/C, especially on the greater current density, suggesting the N-C@CQDs-based battery has better discharge rate performance with 214 mW cm−2 of maximum power density and being greater than the 139 mW cm−2 of the Pt/C-based battery. The batteries were also discharged at different current densities. As displayed in Fig. 4d, the voltages of the two batteries are similar at lower current densities. However, the voltage of N-C@CQDs-based battery at 50 mA cm−2 is obviously higher than that of Pt/C-based battery, further demonstrating the higher rate performance of the N-C@CQDs catalyst. The batteries were galvanostaticly discharged at 50 mA cm−2 until the zinc sheet was completely depleted, and the volumetric energy density was calculated according to the mass loss of zinc. As shown in Fig. 4e, the N-C@CQDs-based battery can stably discharged at ∼ 1.22 V with 959 Wh kg-1 Zn, being higher than the ∼ 1.05 V and 770 Wh kg-1 Zn of the Pt/C-based battery. The durability of N-C@CQDs in zinc air battery was also measured at 50 mA cm−2 by mechanically replacing the anode zinc sheets for almost 120 h. As observed in Fig. 4f, just a slight declining occurred on the gravimetric energy density, evidencing the superior ORR activity can be achieved in the practical applications.

A rechargeable zinc air battery was assembled by mixing N-C@CQDs with RuO2 to assess the cycling stability of the catalyst. As shown in Fig. 5 a-b, the zinc-air battery of N-C@CQDs + RuO2 has a high-power density (152 mW cm−2) and can maintain a stable discharge and recharge voltages (1.17 V, 1.99 V, and 0.82 V of difference), exceeding 87 mW cm−2 and 0.90 V of the Pt/C + RuO2 batteries, and showing the amazing cyclic durability of N-C@CQDs catalyst. The practical exhibition of N-C@CQDs-based battery was also compared with other catalysts recently reported based on the specific capacity and peak power density in Fig. 5c, in which the N-C@CQDs ranks in the first row.

In summary, a high-performance ORR catalyst named N-C@CQDs was prepared by using a low eutectic point complex salt ZnCl2-NH4Cl (179 °C) as a pore regulator and K2FeO4 as a graphitization catalyst to activate the cellulose. The catalyst shows a 0.85 V of ORR half-wave potential, high limited diffusion current as well as high stability, being comparable with the exhibition of Pt/C catalyst. The superior catalytic performance of N-C@CQDs can be ascribed to its ultra-high specific surface area, high percentage (98.8 %) of micropores, large quantities of graphitized carbon quantum dots and pyridinic nitrogen as catalytic active sites. Both primary and rechargeable Zn-air batteries assembled with N-C@CQDs also indicate outstanding performance and stability compared with the Pt/C catalyst, evidencing the remarkable potential in the practical applications. This work presents one-step synthesis strategy of carbon quantum dots doped carbon-based catalyst by high-temperature pyrolysis, providing a new idea for the application of carbon quantum dots in the field of ORR catalysts.

Chenyang Shu: Methodology, Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Chaozhong Guo: Conceptualization, Visualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Xinru Xu: Formal analysis. Lingtao Sun: Formal analysis. Yao Liu: Formal analysis. Hongdian Chen: Methodology. Chuanlan Xu: Methodology. Rong Jin: Methodology, Formal analysis, Supervision. Yujun Si: Conceptualization, Visualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Haifeng Chen: Conceptualization, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.158604.
